content.text: A platform team that cannot measure adoption has no evidence-based way to prioritise improvements. Without adoption metrics, the team may invest heavily in features that few teams use while missing onboarding friction that is preventing the majority from ever getting started. Platform adoption metrics quantify the health of the relationship between the platform and its internal customers: how many teams are using each component, how deeply they rely on it, and whether they are staying or churning.

Platform adoption follows a funnel from awareness to retained usage. Every eligible team starts at the top; the stages measure how far they progress. The funnel is not linear — teams may skip stages, stall at one stage for months, or regress. The goal of the metrics is to identify where the largest drop-offs are so the platform team can focus friction reduction there.
Engineering teams in the organisation that could use GenAI platform capabilities
Engineering org chart or team registry
Team has visited the Backstage catalog entry or TechDocs page for any platform component
Team has provisioned at least one API key via the platform
Team has made a successful LLM API call via the gateway in a non-local environment
First gateway request with environment != local
LLM gateway request logs, keyed by team API key
Team has made at least one platform API call in the last 7 days (rolling)
Most recent API call timestamp < 7 days ago
Team remained weekly active for 90 consecutive days after first deploy
No WAU gap > 7 days in the 90-day window
Six metrics cover the full adoption lifecycle. Each has a clear definition, a data source, and an owner within the platform team.
Count of teams with ≥ 1 active API key
≥ 60% of eligible teams within 6 months of cohort start
Teams with ≥ 1 platform call in rolling 7 days
Teams with no WAU gap > 7d in their first 90 days post first-deploy
Distinct model IDs called via gateway in the last 30 days
Increases as platform adds model support
Teams using prompt registry / total WAU teams
Computing the Adoption Funnel in Python

The funnel is computed from event logs stored in the data warehouse. Each event type is tagged with a team ID and a timestamp. The following script computes the funnel for a given cohort (teams that provisioned their first API key in a given quarter).

# analytics/adoption_funnel.py
# Compute per-cohort platform adoption funnel from event log.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta
from collections import defaultdict

@dataclass
class FunnelResult:
    cohort_quarter: str
    eligible:       int
    registered:     int
    first_deploy:   int
    wau_30d:        int     # WAU 30 days after first deploy
    retained_90d:   int

def build_funnel(events: list[dict], eligible_teams: set[str],
                 cohort_start: date, cohort_end: date) -> FunnelResult:
    """
    events: list of {"team_id": str, "event_type": str, "ts": date, "env": str}
    event_types: "api_key_provisioned" | "gateway_request" | "eval_request" | "prompt_fetch"
    """
    # Index events by type and team
    by_team = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list))
    for e in events:
        by_team[e["team_id"]][e["event_type"]].append(e)

    registered, first_deploy, wau_30d, retained_90d = set(), set(), set(), set()

    for team_id in eligible_teams:
        team_events = by_team[team_id]

        # Stage: Registered — API key provisioned in cohort window
        key_events = [
            e for e in team_events.get("api_key_provisioned", [])
            if cohort_start <= e["ts"] <= cohort_end
        ]
        if not key_events:
            continue
        registered.add(team_id)
        cohort_date = min(e["ts"] for e in key_events)

        # Stage: First deploy — non-local gateway request after registration
        prod_requests = [
            e for e in team_events.get("gateway_request", [])
            if e["env"] != "local" and e["ts"] >= cohort_date
        ]
        if not prod_requests:
            continue
        first_deploy.add(team_id)
        deploy_date = min(e["ts"] for e in prod_requests)

        # Stage: WAU at 30d — any platform call in days 23–30 post-deploy
        window_start = deploy_date + timedelta(days=23)
        window_end   = deploy_date + timedelta(days=30)
        all_calls = (
            team_events.get("gateway_request", []) +
            team_events.get("eval_request", []) +
            team_events.get("prompt_fetch", [])
        )
        if any(window_start <= e["ts"] <= window_end for e in all_calls):
            wau_30d.add(team_id)

        # Stage: 90-day retention — no gap > 7 days in 90-day window
        ninety_day_calls = sorted(
            e["ts"] for e in all_calls
            if deploy_date <= e["ts"] <= deploy_date + timedelta(days=90)
        )
        if ninety_day_calls:
            gaps = [
                (ninety_day_calls[i+1] - ninety_day_calls[i]).days
                for i in range(len(ninety_day_calls) - 1)
            ]
            if not any(g > 7 for g in gaps):
                retained_90d.add(team_id)

    return FunnelResult(
        cohort_quarter=f"{cohort_start.year}-Q{(cohort_start.month - 1) // 3 + 1}",
        eligible=len(eligible_teams),
        registered=len(registered),
        first_deploy=len(first_deploy),
        wau_30d=len(wau_30d),
        retained_90d=len(retained_90d),
    )

Funnel metrics in aggregate hide improvement over time. Cohort analysis — grouping teams by when they first registered — reveals whether onboarding and retention have actually improved across quarters. If the Q3 cohort shows a higher first-deploy conversion than the Q1 cohort, the platform improvements between those quarters worked.

# analytics/cohort_comparison.py
# Compare first-deploy conversion across registration cohorts.

import httpx

DWH_URL = "https://data-warehouse.internal/query"
TOKEN   = "<vault:genai-platform/dwh-read-token>"

QUERY = """
SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('quarter', first_key_date) AS cohort_quarter,
    COUNT(DISTINCT team_id)               AS registered,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN first_prod_call_date IS NOT NULL
                        THEN team_id END) AS reached_first_deploy,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN first_prod_call_date IS NOT NULL
                                      THEN team_id END)
          / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT team_id), 0), 1) AS conversion_pct,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (
        ORDER BY DATEDIFF('day', first_key_date, first_prod_call_date)
    ) AS median_days_to_deploy
FROM platform_team_funnel
WHERE first_key_date >= DATEADD('month', -12, CURRENT_DATE)
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1
"""

def fetch_cohort_report() -> list[dict]:
    resp = httpx.post(DWH_URL, json={"sql": QUERY},
                      headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {TOKEN}"})
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["rows"]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    rows = fetch_cohort_report()
    print(f"{'Quarter':<12} {'Registered':>12} {'First Deploy':>14} {'Conv%':>8} {'Median Days':>13}")
    for r in rows:
        print(f"{r['cohort_quarter']:<12} {r['registered']:>12} "
              f"{r['reached_first_deploy']:>14} {r['conversion_pct']:>7}% "
              f"{r['median_days_to_deploy'] or 'n/a':>13}")

The overall funnel tells you how many teams are using the platform. Component-level adoption tells you which capabilities are actually being used — a team may be counted as active on the gateway while never touching the eval runner or prompt registry. Component-level gaps are the input to the platform roadmap's new-capability prioritisation.
Teams with ≥ 1 request in rolling 30 days
Usually a leading indicator; low means onboarding is failing
Teams with ≥ 1 eval run in rolling 30 days
Low suggests teams are not writing evals; investigate whether quality bar is clear
Teams with ≥ 1 prompt fetch in rolling 30 days
Low suggests manual prompt management; may need better IDE integration or SDK ergonomics
Backstage template instantiations in rolling 30 days
Low suggests teams are not finding the template or find it doesn't meet their needs
Keys provisioned via portal vs. via manual ticket
Low suggests the provisioning portal has usability issues

The WAU/MAU (weekly active users / monthly active users) ratio measures how consistently teams use the platform within the month. A ratio of 1.0 means every team that used the platform at all this month used it every week. A ratio of 0.25 means teams use it roughly once a month — suggesting the platform is not yet integrated into their regular workflow.

# analytics/stickiness.py
# Compute WAU/MAU stickiness ratio per component.

from datetime import date, timedelta
from collections import defaultdict

def compute_stickiness(events: list[dict], component: str,
                       reference_month_start: date) -> float:
    """
    Returns WAU/MAU stickiness for `component` in the calendar month
    starting at `reference_month_start`.
    Events: {"team_id": str, "component": str, "ts": date}
    """
    month_end = (reference_month_start.replace(day=28) + timedelta(days=4)).replace(day=1)

    # MAU: teams active at any point in the month
    mau_teams = {
        e["team_id"] for e in events
        if e["component"] == component
        and reference_month_start <= e["ts"] < month_end
    }

    if not mau_teams:
        return 0.0

    # WAU: teams active in each calendar week; average across weeks
    week_counts = []
    cursor = reference_month_start
    while cursor < month_end:
        week_end = min(cursor + timedelta(days=7), month_end)
        active_this_week = {
            e["team_id"] for e in events
            if e["component"] == component
            and cursor <= e["ts"] < week_end
        }
        week_counts.append(len(active_this_week))
        cursor = week_end

    avg_wau = sum(week_counts) / len(week_counts)
    return round(avg_wau / len(mau_teams), 2)

# Target: stickiness ≥ 0.5 for core components (gateway, prompt registry)
# A stickiness < 0.3 suggests the component is used occasionally/ad-hoc
# rather than as an integrated part of the team's workflow.

Monthly adoption metrics are shared in the platform digest and the Backstage TechDocs page. The report has a consistent structure so readers can track trends without re-learning the format each month.

# GenAI Platform Adoption Report — June 2026

## Funnel Summary
| Stage            | Teams | MoM change |
|------------------|------:|------------|
| Eligible         |   120 | +5         |
| Registered       |    94 | +8 (+9.3%) |
| First deploy     |    71 | +6 (+9.2%) |
| Weekly active    |    52 | +4 (+8.3%) |
| 90-day retained  |    44 | +3 (+7.3%) |

**Conversion (eligible → first deploy): 59%** ↑ from 55% last month

## Component Adoption (WAU teams)
| Component           | Jun | May | Stickiness |
|---------------------|----:|----:|------------|
| LLM gateway         |  52 |  48 | 0.72       |
| Eval runner         |  31 |  27 | 0.58       |
| Prompt registry     |  24 |  19 | 0.51       |
| Starter templates   |   6 |   4 | n/a (events) |

## Cohort Comparison (Q1 vs Q2 2026)
| Cohort  | Registered | First-deploy % | Median days to deploy |
|---------|------------|----------------|-----------------------|
| Q1-2026 | 38         | 52%            | 6.2 days              |
| Q2-2026 | 56         | 66%            | 3.8 days              |

Q2 cohort shows 14pp improvement in first-deploy conversion and 2.4-day
improvement in time-to-first-deploy. Primary driver: self-serve API key
provisioning launched April 15, eliminating the manual ticket step.

## Notable Trends
- Prompt registry adoption crossed 50% of active teams for the first time.
  Likely driver: VS Code extension released May 28.
- 3 teams churned in June (no WAU for 2+ weeks). All 3 are being contacted
  for qualitative interview to understand blockers.

## Next Actions
- WAU stickiness for eval runner (0.58) is below the 0.6 target. TC @alice
  will do outreach to the 21 registered-but-not-active teams this month.
- Starter template instantiation is low (6 in June). Adding a "Try the
  template" button to the Backstage component page in July sprint.

A team that stops being weekly active after prior engagement is a churn event. Understanding why teams churn is as important as understanding why they adopt. The platform team monitors for churn weekly and reaches out to recently-churned teams.

A team is churned when it has not made any platform call in the last 14 days after previously being weekly active for ≥ 4 weeks. Shorter windows create false churn signals (a team on vacation for 10 days is not churned).
Reach out within 1 week of churn.

A TC sends a short Slack message: "We noticed your team hasn't used the platform recently — is there something we can help with?" This is not a complaint or an accusation; it is an offer of support. Most teams respond with a specific friction point that can be addressed.
A churned team that returns to weekly active within 60 days is a re-engagement event. Re-engagement rate is a lagging indicator of how effective the outreach and friction fixes have been.
After 3 months of churn interviews, cluster the reasons into themes and add the top themes to the friction backlog as P2 items with the evidence from the interviews as signal.

Platform adoption metrics answer the question that the platform team cannot afford to leave unanswered: are developers actually using what we build, and are they staying? The adoption funnel — from eligible to registered to first deploy to weekly active to retained — localises where teams are getting stuck. Cohort analysis reveals whether investments are improving adoption over time. Component-level adoption metrics show which capabilities are underused relative to the active team base. WAU/MAU stickiness distinguishes integrated daily use from occasional visits. Churn monitoring with proactive outreach closes the feedback loop between lost adoption and the friction fixes that bring teams back. Together, these metrics give the platform team the same evidence base that a product team has for understanding whether its product is succeeding.
